summary: Detected 6 brute-force attack source(s) targeting this SSH server. The most aggressive attacker was 183.62.140.253 with 149 failed attempts. Attackers primarily targeted common usernames (root, admin, etc.) indicating automated botnet/script activity. Reverse DNS mismatches detected on 1 source(s), suggesting spoofed or compromised hosts.
recommendations: Immediately block all identified brute-force source IPs using iptables, fail2ban, or cloud security groups
Enable fail2ban with a low threshold (e.g., 5 failures = 1 hour ban) for SSH to automatically block repeat attackers
Disable SSH password authentication entirely and enforce key-based authentication only (PasswordAuthentication no)
Disable direct root login over SSH (PermitRootLogin no) or restrict it to specific users
Move SSH to a non-standard port to reduce noise from opportunistic scanners
Implement rate-limiting on SSH connections using iptables (e.g., max 3 new connections per minute per IP)
Enable SSH logging to a remote syslog server for tamper-resistant audit trail
Consider deploying an intrusion prevention system (IPS) or using TCP wrappers (hosts.allow/hosts.deny)
Review and enforce strong password policies for any accounts that still allow password login
Monitor auth.log continuously and set up real-time alerts for repeated authentication failures
